attributes.stem:
A 48-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with gradual onset of blurred vision and difficulty seeing at night over the past six months. He reports increasing fatigue, joint pain, and darkening of his skin over the past year. He has recently been diagnosed with type 2 diabetes. He has no other medical history and takes metformin. His parent died of liver cancer at age 55.

On examination, he appears well. His vital signs are within normal limits. He has generalised bronze skin pigmentation and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints. Ophthalmological examination reveals bilateral retinal degeneration with areas of atrophy and pigmentary changes.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features suggestive of hereditary haemochromatosis, including bronze diabetes (diabetes mellitus with skin pigmentation), arthropathy, and ocular manifestations. The retinal degeneration is a less common but recognised manifestation of haemochromatosis, occurring due to iron deposition in the retinal pigment epithelium. Patients may present with brown-coloured deposits, or more rarely blurry vision or photopsia.

HFE gene testing is the most appropriate next investigation as it will confirm the diagnosis of hereditary haemochromatosis. The most common mutations are C282Y and H63D. Genetic confirmation is important for both the patient and family members who may require screening.

Liver biopsy was historically used to diagnose haemochromatosis but is now reserved for assessing the degree of fibrosis in patients with established disease. It is more invasive and carries risks that are unnecessary when genetic testing is available.

Retinal angiography might help characterise the retinal changes but would not establish the underlying diagnosis. The retinal findings are secondary to systemic iron overload and treating the primary condition is the priority.

Oral glucose tolerance test is unnecessary as the patient already has established diabetes. The focus should be on confirming the underlying cause of his constellation of symptoms.

Skin biopsy would show increased melanin and iron deposition but is not necessary for diagnosis when more specific tests are available. The bronze discolouration is a characteristic clinical finding in haemochromatosis.

Early diagnosis of haemochromatosis is crucial as regular phlebotomy can prevent progression of organ damage, though existing damage may be irreversible. The family history of early liver cancer adds weight to the diagnosis, as hepatocellular carcinoma is a known complication of untreated haemochromatosis.
learningPoints: Bronze diabetes (haemochromatosis with skin pigmentation and diabetes) can present with retinal degeneration.
Early diagnosis of haemochromatosis is crucial as organ damage is preventable but irreversible once established.
references: Winters J, Melachuri S, Wells J, Afshar A.  Ocular manifestations of Haemochromatosis and Iron-overloaded states.  Eye Wiki. Available at: https://eyewiki.org/Ocular_Manifestations_of_Hemochromatosis_and_Iron-Overloaded_States

Shahandeh A, Bui BV, Finkelstein DI, Nguyen CTO. Effects of Excess Iron on the Retina: Insights From Clinical Cases and Animal Models of Iron Disorders. Frontiers in Neuroscience. 2022.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8851357/
